channel.title: The Grant Desk - AI & Safety, added in last 30 days
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: AI & Safety, added in last 30 days.

item.title: GovAI Research Fellow (AI Governance) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: London, UK or Washington, DC preferred; open to candidates based elsewhere. UK visa sponsorship available; US sponsorship only in exceptional cases.

Award: Full-time 2-year fixed-term contract, renewable. Salary by experience and location: London typically GBP 85,000-110,000; Washington DC typically USD 110,000-180,000, with additional compensation for higher cost-of-living regions such as the SF Bay Area. Benefits: health, dental and vision insurance; GBP 5,000 (~USD 6,000) annual wellbeing budget; GBP 5,000 (~USD 6,000) annual professional-development budget; ~USD 500/month coworking for remote staff; flexible hours; extended parental leave; 10% employer pension contribution; 33 days paid vacation (including public holidays). No application fee.

Duration: 2-year full-time contract, renewable

Deadline: 17 Aug 2026

Research Fellows play a central role in GovAI's research: experienced researchers who conduct independent, high-quality work with direct relevance to AI governance and policy while contributing to GovAI's intellectual community. NOTE: applications open at the end of July 2026; GovAI does not publish a fixed closing date, so the 17 August 2026 date shown here is just when this listing is set to expire (check the GovAI page for the live status). Work may take the form of academic publications, policy memos, reports or blog posts, alongside mentoring early-career researchers (such as Summer and Winter Fellows), shaping GovAI's research strategy and convening stakeholders. Fellows work under a Senior Research Fellow or leadership-team member but have substantial autonomy over projects, agendas and publication formats. TOPICS include risk management, threat modeling, economics of AI, geopolitics of AI, public policy and technical governance; candidates working on adjacent topics are also welcome. ELIGIBILITY: open to a wide range of academic and professional backgrounds (computer science, political science, public policy, economics, philosophy, law, plus government/industry/civil-society experience); no strict educational requirement, but the strongest candidates typically have multiple years of relevant research or policy experience and a track record of high-quality, impactful work. A shared application also covers a one-year visiting Research Scholar position for those with less experience (apply once for both). PROCESS: written application; a paid remote work test (some workstreams add a research agenda and a second paid test); then a final stage of two interviews and reference checks. Deep expertise in technical governance, frontier safety design, cybersecurity or US policy is especially welcome. Contact recruitment@governance.ai with location or access questions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: IAS Civic Science Fellow: AI and Society (Nelson Lab) 2026-27
item.description:
Organisation: Institute for Advanced Study (IAS), Science, Technology, and Social Values Lab

Location: Princeton, NJ, USA (in-person)

Award: USD 7,277.77 per month (about USD 87,300/year) for the 18-month full-time appointment, plus a comprehensive benefits package (medical, dental and vision insurance; life and short/long-term disability coverage; flexible spending accounts; paid holidays; retirement plans; tuition assistance; employee assistance program). No application fee.

Duration: 18-month full-time fellowship (2026-27)

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Institute for Advanced Study is seeking a 2026-27 Civic Science Fellow to advance understanding of the social, democratic and scientific implications of artificial intelligence. The full-time, 18-month position is based in the Science, Technology, and Social Values Lab led by Harold F. Linder Professor of Social Science Alondra Nelson. Working closely with Professor Nelson and Lab members, the Fellow contributes to research and policy analysis and to scholarly and public-facing publications, and supports multidisciplinary, multisector initiatives at the intersection of AI, science and governance. DUTIES (75% Lab work): conduct original research on the Lab's AI-and-society agenda; synthesize scholarship on the social implications of AI, sociotechnical approaches to AI governance and the epistemological implications of AI in research; contribute policy research on governance mechanisms that strengthen democratic institutions; help organize convenings and working groups; collaborate on manuscripts, reports and grant proposals; support editorial development of publications; and develop public-facing essays, policy briefs and digital materials. The remaining 25% supports the Fellow's own independent research, network-building and full participation in the intellectual life of IAS. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: a PhD in the social sciences, sciences or humanities; a strong record of scholarly research, writing and publication on the social implications of science and technology or related fields; and exceptional writing and editing skills for both academic and non-academic audiences. Preferred: specialization in science and technology studies, science and technology policy, organizational studies or qualitative methods, and demonstrated engagement with AI governance and technology policy debates. Apply with a cover letter and resume via the IAS/Paylocity posting.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Manifund: AI Safety Regranting (Rolling)
item.description:
Organisation: Manifund

Location: Remote; US-based platform, open worldwide.

Award: Grants typically USD 5,000 to 50,000, recommended by independent regrantors from their own budgets. No application fee. No legal entity required.

Duration: Varies by project (rolling)

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Manifund's regranting program funds AI safety and global-catastrophic-risk work through a network of expert regrantors, each given an independent budget to recommend grants to people and projects they find promising. Applicants submit a project proposal on the Manifund platform; regrantors can then offer funding. GRANTS: typically USD 5,000 to 50,000, though amounts vary. ELIGIBILITY: individuals (no legal entity or institutional affiliation required) and organizations working on AI safety or reducing catastrophic risk from AI. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis; per-regrantor budget availability varies over time. Learn more at the regranting page and apply on the Manifund platform (manifund.org).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Paid Research Assistant: AI and the War in Ukraine
item.description:
Organisation: Bogna Konior (NYU Shanghai)

Location: Remote-friendly.

Award: USD 18 per hour; 10-15 hours per week, flexible schedule.

Duration: Ongoing part-time research assistantship; start as soon as possible.

Deadline: Closed 23 Jul 2026

Bogna Konior, Assistant Professor of Media Theory at NYU Shanghai, is hiring a paid research assistant for a project on Ukraine and artificial intelligence. The work supports a forthcoming book with MIT Press, plus an independent scenario she is writing on the future of AI with the war as a lens. WHAT THE WORK LOOKS LIKE: going month-by-month through news archives (New York Times, Reuters, Financial Times, and specialist outlets), annotating and cataloguing coverage of AI, drones, and autonomous systems in the war, plus other developments she will direct you to; building and maintaining a structured Notion database covering chronology, companies, industries, and key actors; and organizing and editing her existing notes. WHO SHE IS LOOKING FOR: interest in and general knowledge of AI; prior research and annotation experience, careful and detail-oriented, no sloppy citations; comfortable with Notion and with AI tools; Ukrainian language skill a strong plus (not required); familiarity with AI, war, tech, and geopolitics news sources is great. Remote-friendly, start ASAP. She will also consider people with no prior experience, as well as current and previous students. TERMS: USD 18 per hour, 10-15 hours per week, flexible schedule. TO APPLY: email bogna@nyu.edu with a short introduction and background (link to website, socials, CV, LinkedIn, or portfolio if you have one); where you are based and your weekly availability; and one recent AI-related story, company, or development you find interesting and why (a vibe check). Bonus: a sample of past research or writing if you have it.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AGI Governance Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Johns Hopkins University, School of Government and Policy (SOGP)

Location: Hopkins Bloomberg Center, Washington, D.C. (in-person only)

Award: Fully funded: travel, accommodation, and meals covered.

Duration: Three-week in-person intensive, 8-25 September 2026

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

The AGI Governance Fellowship is the inaugural three-week intensive run by Johns Hopkins University's School of Government and Policy (SOGP) to develop the next generation of leaders in AI governance. COVERS: frontier AI legislation at state and federal levels; building societal resilience amid rapid capability growth; democratic practices in AGI alignment; and designing new governing institutions for the AGI era. WHO SHOULD APPLY: early-career researchers and practitioners (within roughly eight years of undergrad) with solid foundations in AI governance, including congressional staffers, AGI company employees, recent CS or policy PhDs, and nonprofit/civil society professionals; a postgraduate degree or equivalent experience is required. This is a capstone program for rising leaders, not an intro course. FUNDING: travel, accommodation, and meals are fully covered. DATES: 8-25 September 2026 at the Hopkins Bloomberg Center in Washington, D.C. (in-person only). DEADLINE: apply by 15 July 2026 for full consideration; rolling applications accepted. APPLY: https://sogp.jh.edu/agi-governance-fellowship/

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Horizon Institute for Public Service: AI Rapid Response Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Horizon Institute for Public Service

Location: Washington, DC area, United States (in-person; full-time placements in U.S. executive branch offices).

Award: Fellow track: USD 170,000 to 200,000+, plus an additional stipend for benefits. Senior Fellow track: from USD 250,000+, plus a benefits stipend, commensurate with the seniority of the placement.

Duration: One year full-time, with the opportunity for renewal.

Deadline: Closed 22 Jul 2026

The AI Rapid Response Fellowship is a focused effort by the Horizon Institute for Public Service to close the AI security talent gap in the U.S. federal government. Horizon is recruiting a specialized cohort of exceptional technical and policy talent to work full-time in executive branch offices on AI security-related issues, with placements beginning as soon as possible. TRACKS: the Fellow track is for candidates with technical or policy expertise relevant to AI security, typically 2-6+ years of full-time relevant experience; the Senior Fellow track is for candidates with substantial prior experience, typically 10-15+ years in relevant technical, policy, or leadership roles. FOCUS: AI governance, AI security, policy, and public service. COMPENSATION: Fellow track USD 170,000-200,000+ plus a benefits stipend; Senior Fellow track from USD 250,000+ plus a benefits stipend, commensurate with the seniority of the placement. DURATION: one year full-time, with the opportunity for renewal, based in the Washington, DC area. ELIGIBILITY: U.S. citizenship is required. An existing security clearance, or eligibility to obtain one, is helpful but not required. DEADLINE: applications are due July 22, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Google Data Center Community AI Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Google (with Watson Institute)

Location: Google Data Center communities, United States. Fellowship delivered with mentorship and training.

Award: Fully funded fellowship including AI training, mentorship, Google resources, and a USD 1,000 community development stipend. No application fee.

Duration: Fellowship program with AI training and mentorship (dates per program).

Deadline: Closed 12 Jul 2026

The Google Data Center Community AI Fellowship is a fully funded fellowship for community leaders and entrepreneurs using AI or technology to create local impact in Google Data Center communities. Fellows receive AI training, mentorship, access to Google resources, and a USD 1,000 community development stipend to support projects that deliver local impact. WHO IT'S FOR: community leaders and entrepreneurs applying AI or technology to community-level challenges in Google Data Center communities. WHAT'S OFFERED: AI training, mentorship, Google resources, and a USD 1,000 stipend. PRIORITY DEADLINE: 12 July 2026. TO APPLY: submit the application via the Watson Institute program page.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AIxDESIGN x KB: Open Call for Sessions, (Art)works and Performances
item.description:
Organisation: AIxDESIGN, co-presented with the Koninklijke Bibliotheek (KB), National Library of the Netherlands

Location: KB, The Hague, Netherlands (in-person exhibition and programme); pitches welcome from people based anywhere

Award: Fees by track: opening-night experience EUR 1,500 to 2,000; a session in the events programme EUR 2,000 to 2,500; an existing (art)work for the exhibition EUR 200 to 400 honorarium (including licensing and production). The fee stays the same wherever you are based.

Duration: Exhibition runs November 2026 to February 2027; opening event November 2026

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

AIxDESIGN and the KB (Koninklijke Bibliotheek, the National Library of the Netherlands) are building an exhibition and public programme about AI, made from the national collection, at the KB in The Hague. Nadia Piet is Creative Lead with the AIxDESIGN community, co-presented with the KB. The premise: long before neural nets, libraries were the machines we built to organise human knowledge, and the show uses the KB's collection to look at what AI is, where it comes from, and how it connects to the older story of how we store, sort, and search what we know. Part of the show lives in the KB Atelier, with talks, workshops, and screenings alongside, intended as AI literacy you can wander into. WHAT THEY WANT: work that engages AI in some way (critical, creative, hands-on, or playful); bonus if it also speaks to archives, libraries, or how we store and sort knowledge, though you do not need to touch the KB's collection to take part. You may pitch to more than one track: (A) SOMETHING FOR THE OPENING NIGHT, a performance, talk, screening, tour, ritual, booth, game, or other experience for the November 2026 opening, fee EUR 1,500 to 2,000; (B) A SESSION IN THE EVENTS PROGRAMME, a workshop or other session once during the three-month run, around 2.5 to 4 hours, fee EUR 2,000 to 2,500; (C) AN (ART)WORK FOR THE EXHIBITION, existing work in any format (installation, sculpture, zine stand, a corner of your own) sturdy enough for a public area with daily traffic, honorarium EUR 200 to 400 including licensing and production; (D) SOMETHING ELSE ENTIRELY, pitch it anyway. WHO: artists, designers, researchers, technologists, performers, musicians, archivists, or educators working around AI, data, libraries, or cultural heritage; no finished idea or polished CV needed, just a clear, specific pitch. You can be based anywhere and they especially welcome people outside the Netherlands; the show is in-person in The Hague and the fee stays the same wherever you are coming from. HOW TO APPLY: send a short pitch through the form (which track, your idea in a paragraph or two, roughly what it would need, and a link to your work); takes about five minutes, no full application required. Deadline: end of August 2026. Questions to hello@aixdesign.co.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: The Singapore AI Safety Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Singapore AI Safety Hub (SASH)

Location: Singapore (three-month in-person fellowship, 21 September - 4 December 2026)

Award: Stipend of SGD 5,000 per month, plus housing and travel. Up to USD 30,000 in compute per project. Includes mentorship, research management support, and central office space in Chinatown.

Duration: Three months in Singapore, 21 September to 4 December 2026

Deadline: Closed 10 Jul 2026

The Singapore AI Safety Fellowship is an independent research fellowship that connects fellows with leading mentors working on technical AI safety and governance. Fellows conduct research for three months in Singapore (21 September to 4 December 2026), building the skills and relationships for international coordination on AI safety. It is built for researchers who want their work to inform real-world AI safety practice on top of publishing it, drawing on Singapore's role as a globally connected, policy-engaged hub. The program rests on three pillars: cross-regional collaboration (bringing together researchers from East and West to build trusted relationships for sustained cross-border AI safety collaboration); technical depth and research excellence (guided by experienced researchers and advisors, fellows investigate pressing challenges in making frontier AI systems safe, secure, and trustworthy); and applying research to policy (institutional partnerships that ground projects in real-world application and help fellows translate technical ideas into practical frameworks and policy recommendations). Advisors include Zhang Ya-Qin (Chair Professor, Tsinghua University; Founding Dean of the Institute for AI Industry Research) and Ryan Kidd (Co-Executive Director, MATS Program). Fellows receive weekly mentorship from established AI safety researchers, dedicated Research Managers, a stipend of SGD 5,000 per month with housing provided throughout, up to USD 30,000 in compute for projects that require it, central office space at SASH's hub in Chinatown, and a community of talks, workshops, and social events. The application deadline is 10 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AIxDESIGN: Grain, Open Call for Critical AI Literacy Recipes
item.description:
Organisation: AIxDESIGN, co-produced with the Netherlands Film Academy

Location: Remote/online; open to educators and practitioners based anywhere

Award: Need-based fee of EUR 150 per recipe for freelance and independent practitioners (email hello@aixdesign.co if the time would be hard to make without it). Your recipe is formatted and credited to you in the first edition of Grain, plus a print copy and an invite to the launch. Published under CC BY-SA 4.0; you keep authorship.

Duration: One-off contribution; Grain publishes March 2027

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

Grain is a cookbook of critical and creative AI literacy exercises for educators across audiovisual and creative practice, co-produced by AIxDESIGN and the Netherlands Film Academy and led by Nadia Piet. The open call collects 'recipes': tried-and-tested group exercises for the classroom or workshop that get people thinking critically and making creatively with AI. Exercises can be hands-on or analog, 15 minutes or a full day, beginner or advanced; the one hard requirement is that someone else can read your recipe and run it themselves. Examples of what might fit: hands-on experiments with specific AI tools (image generators, chatbots, code assistants, video models); analog activities that reveal computational logic through embodied practice; critical readings, watchings, or listenings with structured discussion; provocations, games, or role-plays that get at how AI sees, thinks, or makes; and exercises that help people figure out how they want to relate to AI in their own practice. WHAT YOU GET: your recipe formatted and credited to you in the first edition of Grain, a print copy when it lands, and an invite to the launch. Everything is published under CC BY-SA 4.0 so the work can travel and you keep authorship. FEE: this round offers a need-based fee for freelance and independent practitioners of EUR 150 per recipe; if making the time would be hard without it, email hello@aixdesign.co with your proposal. After this round the call stays open but the fee will not. LIVE SESSION: an optional 30-minute live session on Wednesday 12 August 2026 at 16:00 CEST lets you bring a rough exercise and turn it into a recipe on the spot with the Grain Recipe Converter (online, sign up at luma.com/84w148in). Submissions are open until the end of August 2026; Grain publishes March 2027. Submit via the form; questions to hello@aixdesign.co.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Genesis Mission AI Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Horizon Institute for Public Service (with Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science)

Location: United States. Fellows are forward deployed to US Department of Energy national labs (initial partners: Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory). Remote or in-person; on-site travel at least once a quarter. Must be eligible to work in the US (no visa sponsorship; US green card holders eligible).

Award: Prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000.

Duration: 9-12 months, starting fall 2026.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Horizon Institute for Public Service, Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science are partnering to launch a fellowship that embeds technical AI talent across the US Department of Energy national labs to advance the goals of the Genesis Mission, DOE's flagship science and technology initiative launched in late 2025. The Genesis Mission identifies 26 national science and technology challenges critical to US leadership and envisions a national platform connecting supercomputers, experimental facilities, AI systems, and unique datasets, with DOE's 17 national labs playing a key role. Genesis Mission AI Fellows are forward deployed to DOE national labs to bring frontier AI expertise to high-priority scientific challenges. As core members of the technical staff on a Genesis Mission project, fellows work alongside national lab scientists and partners and apply AI to national priorities, from building cutting-edge models of plasma dynamics in pursuit of controlled fusion to advancing state-of-the-art models for foundational materials discovery. Initial national lab partners include Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, and the Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory; additional partners may be announced later. COMPENSATION/DURATION: 9-12 months starting fall 2026, with a prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000; fellows are expected to travel to their national lab site at least once a quarter but may work on-site if they prefer. ELIGIBILITY: competitive applicants should have at least 2 years of industry experience building AI models or agents (those with industry experience building AI applications or data infrastructure will also be considered); scientific domain expertise is not required, but any placement will involve some training and study in a specific scientific discipline or energy topic, supported by expert colleagues at the host institution. All fellows must be eligible to work in the United States for the duration of the placement; US employment visas cannot be sponsored, but US permanent residents (green card holders) are eligible. APPLICATION: initial form requires a CV or resume (max 2 pages) and short-answer questions on motivations and experiences (max 200 words); successful applicants complete interviews through partner organizations and national labs, and selected fellows are matched to partner labs by scientific domain and availability. The review uses evidence-based best practices including blind grading of application materials. TIMELINE: application deadline 31 July 2026; final fellow selection August-September 2026; expected start fall 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: Washington, D.C., USA (hybrid; remote participation available)

Award: Free to participate; in-person attendees provided dinner (value kept at or below USD 20 per session)

Duration: 12-week part-time program, early September to early December 2026 (approximately 5 hours per week)

Deadline: Closed 05 Jul 2026

The GovAI U.S. AI Policy Program is a bipartisan, part-time, 12-week program designed to accelerate or launch careers in U.S. AI policy. Open to U.S. policy professionals across government and think tanks, it builds a technically-informed understanding of the AI policy landscape through guided discussion sessions, with an emphasis on general-purpose AI. Topics include how general-purpose AI systems are developed; inputs to AI development such as energy, chips, and data centers; AI's effects on productivity, scientific discovery, and economic growth; national security and great power competition; corporate self-governance; AI regulation in other jurisdictions; and federal and state AI regulatory proposals in the U.S. Participants also have weekly opportunities to question world-leading experts; prior guest speakers have included White House OSTP staffers, former frontier-lab employees, and people who helped draft U.S. state- and federal-level AI policy proposals. The program runs early September to early December 2026 (break for Thanksgiving week) with a roughly 5-hour weekly commitment: two hours of synchronous evening sessions (EST), held in-person at GovAI's D.C. office near DuPont Circle or remotely with cameras on, plus three hours of independent reading and podcasts from a free syllabus. To list completion on a resume, participants must attend 9 of 12 sessions, write a short policy memo or op-ed, and pass an interview-based exam. Eligible participants include executive and legislative branch staff (fellows, interns, detailees, military personnel, national laboratory personnel) and think-tank policy researchers and affiliates, across a range of seniority levels, with preference for the D.C. metropolitan area; participants need not currently cover AI but should aspire to a career in U.S. tech or national security policy. Selection has two stages: an online application with six biographical questions, a resume, two verbal reasoning questions, and an approximately 7-minute video; followed by a 30-minute interview in July, with final decisions in early August.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Technology Fellow: AI and Philanthropy
item.description:
Organisation: Ford Foundation

Location: New York, New York, USA

Award: USD 166,000 to 170,000 annual salary, plus comprehensive benefits and retirement contributions of up to 13%

Duration: Two-year term-limited contract

Deadline: Closed 06 Jul 2026

The Ford Foundation seeks a two-year Technology Fellow for AI and Philanthropy, an internally-facing role focused on cross-programmatic learning and advisory support. Reporting to the Director, Technology and Society, the AI Tech Fellow will support Foundation staff in building and adapting internal frameworks for evaluating AI use in ways that prioritize mission and strategy and address ethical, sociotechnical, and operational considerations. Responsibilities include designing and curating hands-on trainings, workshops, and learning sessions to help staff build practical familiarity with AI tools and critically assess their value and efficacy; creating accessible documentation and guides on mission-aligned AI experimentation; curating sociotechnical conversations on how philanthropy and the nonprofit sector are experimenting with AI in ways that respect privacy, safety, and equity; monitoring emerging global AI developments and providing timely briefings to program teams; conducting landscape research on AI initiatives in the philanthropy and social justice sectors; and building relationships with AI researchers, practitioners, grantees and partners working on mission-enabling AI. The Technology Fellows Program is administered by Ford's Technology and Society Program and is designed to increase sociotechnical capacity within the Foundation. Requirements include a minimum of 7 years working within public-interest, government, civil society, or commercial technology sectors; a baccalaureate degree or equivalent experience; demonstrated sociotechnical expertise in artificial intelligence and related technologies including machine learning systems and generative AI and their societal implications; and experience designing, implementing or supporting AI applications and tools in a complex global setting. Hybrid role based in New York (three days per week in office). Not eligible for visa sponsorship; candidates must be authorized to work in the United States. Submit both a resume and a cover letter to be considered.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).
